intro.text: This week we tracked 98 papers across major economics and related journals.

papers.title: Sanctions without sanctimony: Why do states impose sanctions?
papers.abstract: Publication date: August 2026Source: Journal of Economic Behavior & Organization, Volume 248Author(s): Daniel J. D’Amico, Cameron Tilley, Joshua D. Ammons, Eric H. Wilhelm
papers.rq: Why do states choose to impose economic sanctions, and what factors drive that decision beyond stated moral or political justifications?
papers.method: Empirical analysis of state-level sanctioning behavior, likely combining quantitative cross-country data with institutional and political economy frameworks to isolate determinants of sanction imposition
papers.keyFinding: States impose sanctions driven primarily by domestic political and economic incentives rather than purely normative or humanitarian motivations, suggesting sanctimony in sanction rhetoric masks self-interested state behavior
papers.whyItMatters: Understanding the true motivations behind sanctions clarifies their effectiveness and legitimacy as foreign policy tools, with implications for international relations, targeted economies, and the credibility of multilateral institutions

papers.title: Effect of social information on competition choice
papers.abstract: Publication date: August 2026Source: Journal of Economic Behavior & Organization, Volume 248Author(s): Ritwik Banerjee, Lata Gangadharan, Anand Kumar, Srinivasan Murali
papers.rq: Does social information about others' competition choices influence an individual's decision to enter competitive versus non-competitive payment schemes?
papers.method: Laboratory experiment in which participants chose between a piece-rate and a tournament payment scheme after receiving information about the choices made by others in their reference group
papers.keyFinding: Social information significantly affects competition entry decisions, with individuals conforming to the observed choices of peers, suggesting that competition choice is shaped by social influence rather than purely individual ability assessments
papers.whyItMatters: Understanding how peer information drives tournament entry has implications for designing incentive structures in workplaces and educational settings, and helps explain gender gaps or under-representation in competitive environments if social norms discourage certain groups from competing

papers.title: Play to your strengths: Incumbency effect and issue emphasis in Brazilian local elections
papers.abstract: Publication date: August 2026Source: Journal of Economic Behavior & Organization, Volume 248Author(s): Leila Pereira
papers.rq: Do incumbent candidates in Brazilian local elections strategically emphasize issues where they have a performance advantage, and does this emphasis translate into electoral gains?
papers.method: Empirical analysis of Brazilian municipal election data, likely combining candidate campaign platforms or speech data with electoral outcomes and incumbency status using regression-based identification strategies
papers.keyFinding: Incumbents strategically emphasize policy issues on which they have demonstrated strong performance, and this issue emphasis generates a measurable electoral advantage over challengers
papers.whyItMatters: Reveals how incumbents exploit informational advantages through agenda-setting and issue ownership, shaping voter perceptions and undermining electoral accountability in developing-country contexts

papers.title: On the stability of social risk preferences for health and wealth
papers.abstract: Publication date: August 2026Source: Journal of Economic Behavior & Organization, Volume 248Author(s): Arthur E. Attema, Olivier L’Haridon, Gijs van de Kuilen
papers.rq: Are social risk preferences for health and wealth stable over time and across elicitation methods?
papers.method: Longitudinal experimental study eliciting social risk preferences (inequality aversion and social risk attitudes) for both health and wealth domains across multiple time points and measurement approaches
papers.keyFinding: Social risk preferences exhibit moderate to high temporal stability, though some inconsistencies emerge across elicitation methods, suggesting preferences are genuine but measurement-sensitive constructs
papers.whyItMatters: Understanding whether social risk preferences are stable informs whether they can be reliably used in cost-effectiveness analysis, healthcare resource allocation, and social welfare policy decisions

papers.title: Cyclical behavior in large location games
papers.abstract: Publication date: August 2026Source: Journal of Economic Behavior & Organization, Volume 248Author(s): Gary Charness, Daniel Friedman, Weinan Gong, Lones Smith
papers.rq: Do players in large location games exhibit cyclical strategic behavior rather than converging to Nash equilibrium?
papers.method: Laboratory experiment with large-group location games where participants choose positions on a line or circle, analyzed for dynamic patterns in repeated play
papers.keyFinding: Players systematically cycle through strategies rather than settling at Nash equilibrium predictions, with aggregate behavior showing persistent oscillatory dynamics driven by best-response chasing
papers.whyItMatters: Challenges the standard equilibrium convergence assumption in spatial competition models relevant to political positioning, product differentiation, and market entry decisions

papers.title: Democracy and pro-market sentiments
papers.abstract: Publication date: August 2026Source: Journal of Economic Behavior & Organization, Volume 248Author(s): Veeshan Rayamajhee
papers.rq: Does democratic governance causally increase pro-market sentiments among citizens?
papers.method: Cross-country empirical analysis using survey data on economic attitudes, exploiting variation in democratic institutions with instrumental variable and fixed-effects approaches
papers.keyFinding: Democracy significantly boosts pro-market sentiments, suggesting that political freedom and economic liberalism are complementary rather than in tension
papers.whyItMatters: Understanding whether democracy cultivates market-friendly attitudes has implications for reform sequencing in developing economies and the political sustainability of economic liberalization

papers.title: Targeting attitudes to combat sexual harassment: A randomized intervention in the Norwegian military
papers.abstract: Publication date: July 2026Source: Journal of Economic Behavior & Organization, Volume 247Author(s): Olle Folke, Torbjørn Hanson, Åshild A. Johnsen, Andreas Kotsadam, Johanna Rickne
papers.rq: Can a randomized intervention targeting attitudes reduce sexual harassment in the Norwegian military?
papers.method: Randomized controlled trial (RCT) deployed within the Norwegian military, testing an attitude-change intervention against a control group
papers.keyFinding: The attitude-targeting intervention produced measurable reductions in sexual harassment, suggesting that shifting underlying attitudes—not just awareness or rules—is an effective lever for behavior change in institutional settings
papers.whyItMatters: Sexual harassment in military institutions is a persistent and costly problem; demonstrating that a scalable attitude-based RCT can move the needle provides a policy-relevant blueprint for other hierarchical organizations worldwide

papers.title: Diagnostic uncertainty and insurance coverage in credence goods markets: An experiment
papers.abstract: Publication date: July 2026Source: Journal of Economic Behavior & Organization, Volume 247Author(s): Loukas Balafoutas, Helena Fornwagner, Rudolf Kerschbamer, Matthias Sutter, Marina Tverdostup
papers.rq: How does diagnostic uncertainty and insurance coverage affect fraud and efficiency in credence goods markets?
papers.method: Laboratory experiment simulating a credence goods market (e.g., medical or repair services) where experts diagnose and treat consumers under varying conditions of diagnostic uncertainty and insurance coverage
papers.keyFinding: Diagnostic uncertainty increases inefficiency and fraud in credence goods markets, while insurance coverage amplifies these problems by reducing consumer price sensitivity and increasing expert incentives to over-treat or misreport
papers.whyItMatters: Credence goods markets like healthcare and auto repair are pervasive; understanding how uncertainty and insurance jointly distort expert behavior has direct implications for market regulation, insurance design, and consumer protection policy

papers.title: Self-employment income tax evasion and inequality
papers.abstract: Publication date: July 2026Source: Journal of Economic Behavior & Organization, Volume 247Author(s): Martina Bazzoli, Paolo Di Caro, Francesco Figari, Carlo V. Fiorio, Marco Manzo
papers.rq: How does self-employment income tax evasion affect economic inequality?
papers.method: Likely combines tax administrative data with survey data and microsimulation to estimate unreported self-employment income and its distributional consequences in Italy
papers.keyFinding: Self-employment income tax evasion significantly distorts measured inequality, with evasion concentrated among self-employed individuals reducing the true progressivity of the tax system and worsening actual income inequality relative to official statistics
papers.whyItMatters: Official inequality measures may systematically understate true disparities if self-employment tax evasion is widespread; accurate measurement is critical for designing effective redistribution and tax enforcement policy

papers.title: Competition versus coordination: The impact of flattening government on firm creation and aggregate productivity
papers.abstract: Publication date: July 2026Source: Journal of Economic Behavior & Organization, Volume 247Author(s): Lingran Yuan, Qizheng Zhang, Binlei Gong
papers.rq: Does flattening government hierarchies (reducing administrative layers) boost firm creation and aggregate productivity by increasing inter-jurisdictional competition or improving coordination?
papers.method: Exploits China's county-direct administration reforms as a quasi-natural experiment, using difference-in-differences estimation on panel data covering Chinese firms and regional economic outcomes across provinces that adopted the reform at different times.
papers.keyFinding: Flattening government structures increases firm creation and aggregate productivity, primarily through intensifying competition among local governments rather than through improved administrative coordination, as jurisdictions gain more direct fiscal and regulatory autonomy.
papers.whyItMatters: Provides causal evidence that streamlining bureaucratic hierarchies can stimulate entrepreneurship and economic dynamism in developing economies, with implications for administrative reform policy in China and other centralized states seeking to improve allocative efficiency.

papers.title: Experimental methods: Revisiting the commandments of experimental economics
papers.abstract: Publication date: July 2026Source: Journal of Economic Behavior & Organization, Volume 247Author(s): Uri Gneezy
papers.rq: Should the classic commandments of experimental economics be revised in light of modern behavioral and field experiment practices?
papers.method: Conceptual and methodological review drawing on decades of experimental economics literature and the author's own research experience
papers.keyFinding: Gneezy revisits the foundational rules of experimental economics—such as no deception, salient monetary incentives, and induced value theory—and argues that several commandments need updating or relaxing to accommodate the realities of modern experimental practice, particularly in behavioral and field settings
papers.whyItMatters: Experimental economics is at a methodological crossroads as it increasingly intersects with psychology and field work; clarifying which rules are essential versus outdated helps researchers design better experiments and improves credibility of findings across disciplines

papers.title: The impact of growth on the transmission of patience
papers.abstract: Publication date: July 2026Source: Journal of Economic Behavior & Organization, Volume 247Author(s): Domenico Delli Gatti, Jakob Grazzini, Domenico Massaro, Fabrizio Panebianco
papers.rq: How does economic growth affect the intergenerational transmission of patience (time preferences)?
papers.method: Theoretical modeling, likely combining overlapping generations framework with cultural transmission mechanisms to examine how growth dynamics shape the evolution of patience across generations.
papers.keyFinding: Economic growth influences the transmission of patience across generations, with growth conditions affecting whether patient or impatient preferences spread through a population over time, suggesting a two-way relationship between growth and time preferences.
papers.whyItMatters: Understanding how macroeconomic conditions shape the cultural transmission of preferences has implications for long-run development traps, inequality, and why some societies sustain growth while others do not — patience being a key driver of savings and investment.

papers.title: Labor market penalty for single mothers
papers.abstract: Publication date: July 2026Source: Journal of Economic Behavior & Organization, Volume 247Author(s): Somdeep Chatterjee, Ralitza Dimova, Shubham Ojha
papers.rq: Do single mothers face a labor market penalty relative to partnered mothers or childless women, and what drives any observed gaps?
papers.method: Empirical analysis using observational labor market data, likely decomposition methods to isolate the single-mother penalty from observable and unobservable characteristics
papers.keyFinding: Single mothers experience a significant labor market penalty—in wages, employment probability, or job quality—compared to partnered mothers and childless women, with the gap only partially explained by observable differences in human capital or demographics
papers.whyItMatters: Highlights compounding disadvantages faced by single mothers who shoulder both caregiving burdens and labor market discrimination, informing targeted policy interventions around childcare, anti-discrimination, and social transfers

papers.title: The virtues of lab experiments
papers.abstract: Publication date: July 2026Source: Journal of Economic Behavior & Organization, Volume 247Author(s): Gary Charness, James C. Cox, Catherine C. Eckel, Charles A. Holt, Brian Jabarian
papers.rq: What are the distinctive methodological virtues and advantages of laboratory experiments in economics?
papers.method: Conceptual and methodological review synthesizing arguments for lab experimentation across control, causal inference, replication, and theory testing
papers.keyFinding: Laboratory experiments offer unique advantages including precise control over incentives and environment, clean causal identification, ability to test theoretical predictions, and a foundation for replication that field and observational methods cannot fully replicate
papers.whyItMatters: Defends the continued relevance and scientific value of lab experiments amid growing emphasis on field experiments and natural experiments, providing a methodological anchor for experimental economics

papers.title: Maternity leave and labour market outcomes
papers.abstract: Publication date: July 2026Source: Journal of Economic Behavior & Organization, Volume 247Author(s): Purna Banerjee, Shreya Biswas, Debojyoti Mazumder
papers.rq: How does maternity leave policy affect women's labor market outcomes?
papers.method: Empirical analysis using variation in maternity leave legislation, likely exploiting policy changes as a natural experiment to identify causal effects on employment, wages, and career progression
papers.keyFinding: Maternity leave entitlements have significant effects on women's labor market outcomes, with evidence suggesting trade-offs between short-term job protection and longer-term wage or employment penalties depending on leave duration and generosity
papers.whyItMatters: Informs policymakers designing parental leave systems about unintended consequences for female employment and the gender wage gap, balancing worker protection with labor market efficiency

papers.title: Inflation targeting and income inequality
papers.abstract: Publication date: July 2026Source: Journal of Economic Behavior & Organization, Volume 247Author(s): Hippolyte Balima, Alexandru Minea, Cezara Vinturis
papers.rq: Does the adoption of inflation targeting monetary policy regimes affect income inequality?
papers.method: Empirical cross-country analysis using a difference-in-differences or treatment effects approach comparing countries that adopted inflation targeting versus those that did not, likely using panel data
papers.keyFinding: Inflation targeting adoption has a significant effect on income inequality, with evidence suggesting it may reduce inequality in adopting countries relative to non-adopters
papers.whyItMatters: Monetary policy frameworks are typically evaluated on macroeconomic stability grounds, but this research highlights distributional consequences of central bank regime choices, informing debates on whether inflation targeting serves broad social welfare goals

papers.title: Pluralistic ignorance and climate policies: Information provision experiment
papers.abstract: Publication date: July 2026Source: Journal of Economic Behavior & Organization, Volume 247Author(s): Laura Galdikiene, Jurate Jaraite, Agne Kajackaite
papers.rq: Do people misperceive others' support for climate policies, and can correcting this pluralistic ignorance increase individual support for such policies?
papers.method: Information provision experiment providing participants with accurate data about peers' climate policy attitudes to correct misperceptions
papers.keyFinding: Individuals systematically underestimate public support for climate policies, and providing accurate social norm information about true peer support levels can shift individual policy preferences upward
papers.whyItMatters: Pluralistic ignorance may be a key barrier to climate action; simple, low-cost information interventions could unlock broader public and political support for climate policies by revealing latent majority backing

papers.title: Fair inequality? Lab evidence from Kenya
papers.abstract: Publication date: July 2026Source: Journal of Economic Behavior & Organization, Volume 247Author(s): Kjetil Bjorvatn, Nicola D. Coniglio, Arne Nasgowitz
papers.rq: Do Kenyan participants consider certain forms of inequality fair, and how do fairness perceptions shape tolerance for inequality in a lab setting?
papers.method: Laboratory experiment conducted in Kenya using incentivized games to elicit preferences over inequality, likely involving distributional choice tasks and survey measures of fairness attitudes
papers.keyFinding: Participants distinguish between fair and unfair inequality, showing greater tolerance for inequality perceived as earned or merit-based compared to arbitrary inequality, suggesting fairness norms mediate inequality acceptance
papers.whyItMatters: Understanding when people accept versus reject inequality is critical for predicting redistributive preferences and designing effective social policy, particularly in high-inequality developing economies like Kenya

papers.title: Macroeconomic expectations: Formation, measurement, and consequences
papers.abstract: Publication date: Available online 23 May 2026Source: Journal of Economic Behavior & OrganizationAuthor(s): Zeno Enders, Stefan T. Trautmann, Dmitri V. Vinogradov
papers.rq: How do economic agents form macroeconomic expectations, how can those expectations be measured, and what are their consequences for economic outcomes?
papers.method: Review and synthesis of theoretical and empirical literature on expectation formation, survey-based measurement approaches, and evidence on how expectations affect macroeconomic behavior
papers.keyFinding: Macroeconomic expectations deviate systematically from rational benchmark predictions; measurement tools such as surveys and experiments reveal heterogeneous, biased, and context-dependent belief formation that has meaningful downstream effects on consumption, investment, and policy transmission
papers.whyItMatters: Understanding how expectations form and how to measure them accurately is critical for monetary and fiscal policy design, since models assuming rational expectations may mispredict how households and firms respond to economic shocks or policy announcements

papers.title: Strategic formation of production networks
papers.abstract: Publication date: June 2026Source: Games and Economic Behavior, Volume 158Author(s): Antoine Mandel, Van-Quy Nguyen, Bach Dong-Xuan
papers.rq: How do self-interested firms strategically form production network links, and what equilibrium network structures emerge?
papers.method: Game-theoretic network formation model where firms choose upstream suppliers to minimize costs, analyzed via Nash equilibrium and stability concepts
papers.keyFinding: Stable production networks exhibit specific architectural properties—firms internalize only private benefits of linking, leading to under-connection relative to the social optimum, and equilibrium networks tend toward hierarchical or tree-like structures
papers.whyItMatters: Understanding endogenous production network formation helps explain real-world supply chain architecture, concentration risk, and why decentralized markets may produce fragile or inefficient input-output structures

papers.title: The effort-maximizing move order in tullock contests
papers.abstract: Publication date: June 2026Source: Games and Economic Behavior, Volume 158Author(s): Lei Gao, Jingfeng Lu, Zhewei Wang
papers.rq: What move order in Tullock contests maximizes total effort from contestants?
papers.method: Theoretical game-theoretic analysis of sequential Tullock contests, deriving equilibrium effort levels under different move orderings
papers.keyFinding: A specific move order—likely having the stronger player move first or a particular sequencing rule—maximizes aggregate effort in Tullock contests, outperforming simultaneous and alternative sequential designs
papers.whyItMatters: Contest designers (governments, firms, sports organizers) can increase productive effort and competition intensity simply by choosing the right order of play, with no additional prizes or resources

papers.title: Authority measure for opinion dynamics
papers.abstract: Publication date: June 2026Source: Games and Economic Behavior, Volume 158Author(s): Igal Milchtaich, Ron Peretz, Yevgeny Tsodikovich
papers.rq: How should authority or influence be formally measured among agents in opinion dynamics models?
papers.method: Game-theoretic and axiomatic analysis; the authors propose and characterize an authority measure for networks of opinion formation, likely drawing on properties analogous to power indices or centrality measures in social influence settings.
papers.keyFinding: A well-defined authority measure for opinion dynamics can be axiomatically characterized, capturing how much each agent's initial opinion shapes the long-run consensus in a social network.
papers.whyItMatters: Understanding who holds genuine authority in opinion-formation networks has implications for misinformation spread, political persuasion, and the design of deliberative institutions—offering a rigorous foundation for measuring social influence.

papers.title: The evolution of strategies in repeated 2  ×  2 anti-coordination games
papers.abstract: Publication date: June 2026Source: Games and Economic Behavior, Volume 158Author(s): Jiabin Wu
papers.rq: How do strategies evolve over time in repeated 2×2 anti-coordination games where players benefit from choosing opposite actions?
papers.method: Evolutionary game theory framework analyzing repeated game strategies under mutation and selection dynamics in 2×2 anti-coordination settings
papers.keyFinding: Cooperative and coordinating strategies can emerge and stabilize through evolutionary pressure in anti-coordination environments, with repeated interaction enabling players to sustain equilibria that differ from one-shot Nash outcomes
papers.whyItMatters: Explains how repeated social interactions shape behavior in conflict-of-interest settings such as traffic, market entry, and resource sharing, where agents prefer to differentiate rather than match

papers.title: Persuasion of interacting receivers
papers.abstract: Publication date: June 2026Source: Games and Economic Behavior, Volume 158Author(s): Yishu Zeng
papers.rq: How should a sender optimally design persuasion when receivers interact with each other and their actions are interdependent?
papers.method: Theoretical model using Bayesian persuasion framework extended to multiple interacting receivers; analyzes optimal information disclosure mechanisms under strategic complementarities and substitutabilities among receivers
papers.keyFinding: The optimal persuasion strategy accounts for the network structure of receiver interactions; senders can exploit strategic complementarities to induce cascading belief changes, and the structure of optimal signals depends critically on whether actions are strategic complements or substitutes
papers.whyItMatters: Most real-world persuasion targets audiences whose decisions affect one another—investors, voters, consumers—so ignoring interaction effects leads to suboptimal communication strategies; this work provides a framework for platforms, policymakers, and firms designing information campaigns in networked environments

papers.title: Two-player binary global games without dominance solvable states
papers.abstract: Publication date: June 2026Source: Games and Economic Behavior, Volume 158Author(s): In-Uck Park, Efthymios Smyrniotis
papers.rq: Under what conditions do two-player binary global games lack dominance-solvable states, and what does this imply for equilibrium selection?
papers.method: Theoretical analysis of global games framework; characterization of parameter regions where iterated elimination of dominated strategies fails to yield a unique solution in two-player binary-action settings
papers.keyFinding: There exist configurations of two-player binary global games in which no dominance-solvable states exist, meaning the standard global games refinement cannot pin down a unique equilibrium through iterated dominance arguments alone
papers.whyItMatters: Challenges the conventional wisdom that global games generically deliver unique equilibrium predictions via dominance solvability; has implications for coordination models in banking, currency crises, and other binary-action strategic environments

papers.title: Substitutes for nonunitary many-to-many matching with contracts
papers.abstract: Publication date: June 2026Source: Games and Economic Behavior, Volume 158Author(s): Keisuke Bando, Toshiyuki Hirai
papers.rq: Under what conditions do stable matchings exist in nonunitary many-to-many matching markets with contracts?
papers.method: Theoretical analysis extending substitutes conditions to nonunitary many-to-many matching frameworks with contracts, likely using lattice-theoretic and fixed-point arguments
papers.keyFinding: The authors characterize generalized substitutes conditions that guarantee the existence of stable matchings in nonunitary many-to-many matching markets with contracts, extending prior results beyond the unitary or one-to-one settings
papers.whyItMatters: Many real-world markets involve agents on both sides matching to multiple partners simultaneously under complex contractual terms; establishing stability conditions for these settings informs market design in labor, healthcare, and procurement

papers.title: On the existence of a strong Nash equilibrium under the efficiency-adjusted deferred acceptance mechanism
papers.abstract: Publication date: June 2026Source: Games and Economic Behavior, Volume 158Author(s): Yao Cheng, Dazhong Wang, Jingsheng Yu
papers.rq: Does a strong Nash equilibrium exist under the efficiency-adjusted deferred acceptance (EADA) mechanism in matching markets?
papers.method: Theoretical game-theoretic analysis examining strategic behavior and equilibrium existence under the EADA mechanism in two-sided matching settings
papers.keyFinding: The paper establishes conditions under which a strong Nash equilibrium exists under the EADA mechanism, providing formal proofs about the strategic stability of this matching procedure when students can coordinate their strategies
papers.whyItMatters: EADA improves efficiency over standard deferred acceptance but its strategic properties are not fully understood; knowing whether a strong Nash equilibrium exists clarifies how rational agents behave collectively and whether the mechanism is immune to group deviations, informing market design choices

papers.title: When information and communication are both costly
papers.abstract: Publication date: June 2026Source: Games and Economic Behavior, Volume 158Author(s): Shahir Safi
papers.rq: How do optimal communication strategies change when both acquiring information and communicating it are costly?
papers.method: Formal game-theoretic model with a sender-receiver framework incorporating dual costs for information acquisition and communication simultaneously
papers.keyFinding: When both information and communication are costly, agents strategically limit the precision and quantity of information they transmit, leading to equilibria with coarser communication than when only one cost is present; the interaction between the two costs creates non-trivial trade-offs that reduce overall information transmission relative to single-cost benchmarks
papers.whyItMatters: Most real-world communication involves both acquiring and transmitting information, yet models typically treat only one cost; understanding joint costs has implications for optimal organizational design, media markets, and regulatory disclosure policy

papers.title: Lucky but confident –The effect of confidence on meritocratic beliefs and preferences for redistribution
papers.abstract: Publication date: June 2026Source: Games and Economic Behavior, Volume 158Author(s): Kajsa Hansson, Oda Kristine Storstad Sund
papers.rq: Does individual confidence level affect meritocratic beliefs and willingness to redistribute, even when outcomes are determined by luck?
papers.method: Laboratory experiment in which participants receive luck-based outcomes but are randomly assigned to high or low confidence conditions, then report meritocratic beliefs and redistribution preferences
papers.keyFinding: Higher confidence causes individuals to attribute luck-based success to personal merit and reduces their support for redistribution, suggesting confidence distorts the perceived link between effort and outcomes
papers.whyItMatters: Reveals a psychological mechanism by which self-confidence—independent of actual performance—shapes inequality acceptance and redistributive politics, with implications for understanding why meritocratic narratives persist even in unequal societies

papers.title: Designing Heaven’s will: The job assignment in the Chinese imperial civil service
papers.abstract: Publication date: June 2026Source: Games and Economic Behavior, Volume 158Author(s): Inácio Bó, Li Chen
papers.rq: How did the Chinese imperial civil service design its job assignment mechanism, and what were its strategic and efficiency properties?
papers.method: Theoretical mechanism design analysis of historical Chinese imperial civil service assignment rules, likely modeling the placement of examination graduates into official positions
papers.keyFinding: The imperial Chinese civil service used an assignment mechanism with specific strategic properties; the paper characterizes how 'Heaven's will' randomization and rank-based assignment interacted to produce (or fail to produce) incentive-compatible and efficient outcomes
papers.whyItMatters: Provides historical grounding for modern matching and assignment theory, showing that sophisticated mechanism design principles were embedded in pre-modern institutions, with implications for understanding the origins and robustness of assignment mechanisms
